content.problemStatement:
Arun has an integer NNN. His friend likes the number 111, so Arun wants to reduce NNN to 111.

To do so, he can perform the following move several times (possibly, zero):


Pick two integers XXX and YYY such that X+YX+YX+Y is even and XYX^YXY is a divisor of NNN. Then, replace NNN by NXY\dfrac{N}{X^Y}XYN​
Note that at each step, XYX^YXY only needs to be a divisor of the current value of NNN, and not necessarily a divisor of the integer Arun initially started with.

Find the **minimum** number of moves Arun needs to reduce NNN to 111. If it is not possible to reduce NNN to 111 using the given operation, print −1-1−1 instead.


Input Format

The first line of input will contain an integer TTT, denoting the number of test cases. The description of TTT test cases follows.
Each test case consists of a single line of input containing one integer NNN.


Output Format
For each test case, output on a new line the answer — the **minimum** number of moves needed to reduce NNN to 111, or −1-1−1 if it is not possible to do so.
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. Each of the next T lines contains a single integer N (1 ≤ N ≤ 10^18).
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single line containing the minimum number of moves required to reduce N to 1 using the described operation, or -1 if it is impossible.

sampleTestCases.explanation: For N=25 (odd) we can choose X=25, Y=1 (25+1 even) and divide by 25 in one move. For N=24 = 2^3·3, the exponent of 2 is 3 (odd), so it is impossible to eliminate all factors of 2 using only even‑exponent powers of 2.

N=1 needs no moves. N=4 = 2^2, v2=2 (even) and no odd part, so one move with X=2, Y=2 removes the whole number. N=12 = 2^2·3, v2 even, odd part 3>1, so two moves: first divide by 4 (X=2,Y=2) obtaining 3, then divide by 3 (X=3,Y=1).

hidden.label: even with even v2 and odd part

solution.approach:
For a move we choose integers X and Y such that X+Y is even and X^Y divides the current N, then replace N by N / X^Y. Observe:
- If N is odd, we can take X = N, Y = 1 (odd + 1 is even) and divide by N in one move.
- If N is even, X must be even and Y must be even (otherwise X+Y would be odd). Hence X^Y is a power of 2 with an even exponent, i.e. 4^k.
  Therefore only factors of the form 4^k can be removed from the even part of N.
- Let v2 be the exponent of 2 in the prime factorisation of N (the number of trailing zero bits). The operation can only eliminate an even number of 2's at a time, so v2 must be even; otherwise reduction is impossible.
- When v2 is even we can remove all 2's in a single move by choosing X = 2 and Y = v2 (2+v2 is even because v2 is even). After this step the remaining number is odd.
- The odd part (if greater than 1) can be removed in one additional move using X = oddPart, Y = 1.
Thus:
  * If N = 1 → answer 0.
  * Else if N is odd → answer 1.
  * Else (N even): compute v2 = exponent of 2 in N.
    - If v2 is odd → answer -1.
    - If v2 is even:
        • If N / 2^{v2} == 1 → answer 1 (only the power of 4 was present).
        • Otherwise → answer 2 (one move for the power of 4, one move for the odd remainder).
solution.timeComplexity: O(T) – each test case needs only a few arithmetic operations and a count of trailing zeros.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(1) – only a few variables are used per test case.
